policy_statement: By 2026, establish an MOU between the City, state regulators, and utilities stating mutual intent to decommission natural gas transmission and distribution in San Francisco (ES.5-4).
mechanism_description: The City plans to establish an MOU with regulators and utilities expressing intent to decommission natural gas transmission and distribution, which reduces fossil gas use and associated emissions.
primary_causal_pathway: Fossil fuel infrastructure phase-out → ↓ fossil gas delivery/consumption + ↓ fugitive methane → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change: The city, regulators, and utilities commit to retiring/decommissioning natural gas transmission and distribution networks, constraining or ending gas service and enabling end-use electrification/alternative fuels.  
(2) Mediating process: Reduced flow of fossil gas through pipelines lowers upstream-to-downstream methane leakage and reduces end-use combustion of methane (natural gas), cutting associated CO₂ emissions; it can also avoid ongoing lock-in and expansion of gas infrastructure.  
(3) Outcome: Net reduction in GHG emissions (methane and CO₂), yielding climate change mitigation.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced greenhouse gas emissions (CO₂ from combustion and methane leakage) by removing natural gas delivery infrastructure and thereby reducing fossil gas use.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Gas network decommissioning primarily reduces fossil gas use and fugitive methane, which directly lowers GHG emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While the policy can have resource-efficiency co-benefits (e.g., avoiding wasted methane) or resilience effects (e.g., reduced explosion risk), the main climate-relevant endpoint is emissions reduction, which fits Mitigation.  
3) Edge cases applied: If decommissioning were pursued mainly for safety/earthquake resilience with no material emissions impact, categorization could shift, but the stated intent here is explicitly to reduce fossil gas use and associated emissions.  
4) Applies to ALL instances: Any policy sharing this mechanism—retiring gas transmission/distribution to reduce gas consumption/leakage—has its dominant climate impact through lowering GHG emissions, so Mitigation applies broadly.  
5) Secondary subtype deferred: Any finer tagging (e.g., methane-focused vs combustion CO₂-focused, enabling electrification) depends on policy-specific evidence and is deferred to policy-level assessment.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If gas decommissioning is not paired with clean electrification and demand reduction, emissions benefits could be reduced or offset by replacement with other fossil fuels; however, the mechanism as defined targets fossil fuel use reduction and methane leakage avoidance, which remains primarily Mitigation.
key_indicators: “By 2026, establish memorandum of understanding”; “between the City, state regulators, and utilities”; “stating mutual intent”; “de-commission natural gas transmission and distribution”
co_benefits: Improved local air quality; public health benefits; reduced explosion/fire risk from aging gas infrastructure
instance_edge_case_notes: MOU signals intent rather than imposing a binding requirement; classification leans to planning-strategy/enabling rather than regulatory-mandate.
